methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $11M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
Marathon Fusion is a seed-stage nuclear energy company founded by Adam Rutkowski and Kyle Schiller. The company has raised $10.9M to date. The valuation exercise draws from a nuclear energy sector universe of 11 peers—three public companies (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and eight private firms including Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Nano Nuclear Energy.

Stage-progression methodology is applicable here, using the $10.9M raised as a valuation proxy and applying a typical 2.5x multiplier for advancement from seed to the next stage. This method yields an implied valuation of $27.3M. Peer-median triangulation is not viable; while eight nuclear energy peers exist, insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables with similar valuation tiers remain (fewer than three datapoints survive filtering), making sector-wide median analysis unreliable and potentially misleading.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $23M (low) to $31M (high), with a median estimate of $27M. This range is derived from stage-progression alone, reflecting early-stage risk and limited comparable data. Key uncertainties include the lack of narrowly comparable seed-stage peers in nuclear fusion and the company's pre-revenue status, both of which constrain confidence in peer-based validation. Investors should weight stage-typical dilution and technology execution risk heavily in decision-making.
